The **Q Broadway Express**[[3]](#cite_note-timetable-3)[[a]](#cite_note-5) is a [rapid transit](/wiki/Rapid_transit "Rapid transit") service in the [B Division](/wiki/B_Division_(New_York_City_Subway) "B Division (New York City Subway)") of the [New York City Subway](/wiki/New_York_City_Subway "New York City Subway"). Its route emblem, or "bullet", is colored yellow since it is a part of the [BMT Broadway Line](/wiki/BMT_Broadway_Line "BMT Broadway Line") in [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan").

The Q operates 24 hours daily between [96th Street](/wiki/96th_Street_(Second_Avenue_Subway) "96th Street (Second Avenue Subway)") on the [Upper East Side](/wiki/Upper_East_Side "Upper East Side") of Manhattan and [Stillwell Avenue](/wiki/Coney_Island%E2%80%93Stillwell_Avenue_(BMT_Brighton_Line) "Coney Island–Stillwell Avenue (BMT Brighton Line)") in [Coney Island](/wiki/Coney_Island "Coney Island"), [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn"). Daytime service makes express stops in Manhattan (between [57th Street–Seventh Avenue](/wiki/57th_Street%E2%80%93Seventh_Avenue_(BMT_Broadway_Line) "57th Street–Seventh Avenue (BMT Broadway Line)") and [Canal Street](/wiki/Canal_Street_(BMT_Broadway_Line) "Canal Street (BMT Broadway Line)")) and all stops in Brooklyn; overnight service makes all stops along the full route.[[3]](#cite_note-timetable-3)

The Q was originally the [Brooklyn–Manhattan Transit Corporation](/wiki/Brooklyn%E2%80%93Manhattan_Transit_Corporation "Brooklyn–Manhattan Transit Corporation")'s (BMT) **1** service; beginning in 1920, it ran along the Brighton Line in Brooklyn and Broadway Line in Manhattan. In the past, the Q has run many different service patterns in Brooklyn, Manhattan and [Queens](/wiki/Queens "Queens"), both local and express, including **QB** service on the [Manhattan Bridge](/wiki/Manhattan_Bridge "Manhattan Bridge") and **QT** service via the [Montague Street Tunnel](/wiki/Montague_Street_Tunnel "Montague Street Tunnel"). From 1988 to 2001, Q service ran along the [IND Sixth Avenue Line](/wiki/IND_Sixth_Avenue_Line "IND Sixth Avenue Line") in Manhattan, with a bullet colored orange. The Q also ran in Queens at various points, including along the [BMT Astoria Line](/wiki/BMT_Astoria_Line "BMT Astoria Line") to [Astoria–Ditmars Boulevard](/wiki/Astoria%E2%80%93Ditmars_Boulevard_(BMT_Astoria_Line) "Astoria–Ditmars Boulevard (BMT Astoria Line)") on weekdays from 2010 to 2016, along the [IND Queens Boulevard Line](/wiki/IND_Queens_Boulevard_Line "IND Queens Boulevard Line") to [Forest Hills–71st Avenue](/wiki/Forest_Hills%E2%80%9371st_Avenue_(IND_Queens_Boulevard_Line) "Forest Hills–71st Avenue (IND Queens Boulevard Line)") during temporary post-9/11 service reroutes, and along the [IND 63rd Street Line](/wiki/63rd_Street_Lines "63rd Street Lines") to [21st Street–Queensbridge](/wiki/21st_Street%E2%80%93Queensbridge_(IND_63rd_Street_Line) "21st Street–Queensbridge (IND 63rd Street Line)") until 2001. There was also a **<Q>** variant from 2001 to 2004, which ran express on the Brighton Line, used a diamond bullet, and terminated at [Brighton Beach](/wiki/Brighton_Beach_(BMT_Brighton_Line) "Brighton Beach (BMT Brighton Line)") due to construction on the north side of the Manhattan Bridge. On January 1, 2017, the Q was rerouted along the [Second Avenue Subway](/wiki/Second_Avenue_Subway "Second Avenue Subway").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Q_(New_York_City_Subway_service)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### 1878–1920: Original railroad

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Q_(New_York_City_Subway_service)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 1878–1920: Original railroad")]

The predecessor to the subway service known as the Q today was the Brooklyn, Flatbush and Coney Island Railway. On July 2, 1878, this [steam railroad](/wiki/Steam_railroad "Steam railroad") began operations on what would become the [BMT Brighton Line](/wiki/BMT_Brighton_Line "BMT Brighton Line"), from [Prospect Park](/wiki/Prospect_Park_(BMT_Brighton_Line) "Prospect Park (BMT Brighton Line)") to the Brighton Beach Hotel in [Brighton Beach](/wiki/Brighton_Beach "Brighton Beach"), which opened at the same time. The Brighton Beach Hotel was located on [Coney Island](/wiki/Coney_Island "Coney Island") by the [Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean") at the foot of modern-day Coney Island Avenue. Passengers could make connections with the [horsecars](/wiki/Horsecar "Horsecar") of the [Brooklyn City Railroad](/wiki/Brooklyn_City_Railroad "Brooklyn City Railroad") at the Prospect Park terminal.[[5]](#cite_note-6)[[6]](#cite_note-Cudahy_1999-7): 9

On August 19, 1878, service was extended north from Prospect Park along what is today the [BMT Franklin Avenue Line](/wiki/BMT_Franklin_Avenue_Line "BMT Franklin Avenue Line") used by the [Franklin Avenue Shuttle](/wiki/Franklin_Avenue_Shuttle "Franklin Avenue Shuttle"), to Atlantic Avenue west of Franklin Avenue, a location known as Bedford station on what is today the [Atlantic Branch](/wiki/Atlantic_Branch "Atlantic Branch") of the [Long Island Rail Road](/wiki/Long_Island_Rail_Road "Long Island Rail Road") (LIRR).[[6]](#cite_note-Cudahy_1999-7): 11 A physical connection existed between the Brighton, Flatbush and Coney Island Railway and the LIRR. By mutual agreement trains of the Brooklyn, Flatbush and Coney Island Railway ran on LIRR trackage west to its terminal at [Flatbush Avenue](/wiki/Atlantic_Terminal "Atlantic Terminal") and Atlantic Avenue, providing a connection to [Downtown Brooklyn](/wiki/Downtown_Brooklyn "Downtown Brooklyn") and ferries to Manhattan. LIRR trains also operated to Brighton Beach from Flatbush Avenue and from its own terminal in [Long Island City](/wiki/Long_Island_City "Long Island City"), with ferry access to [Midtown Manhattan](/wiki/Midtown_Manhattan "Midtown Manhattan"). Initially, service operated during the summer season only. At the end of the 1882 summer season, the LIRR abrogated its agreement allowing Brighton Line trains to access its Flatbush Avenue terminal and beginning with the 1883 summer season, only Brooklyn, Flatbush and Coney Island trains operated between Bedford Terminal and Brighton Beach.

In 1896, a short elevated extension of the Brighton Beach Line (since reorganized as the Brooklyn & Brighton Beach Railroad) opened to the corner of [Franklin Avenue and Fulton Street](/wiki/Franklin_Avenue%E2%80%93Fulton_Street_(New_York_City_Subway) "Franklin Avenue–Fulton Street (New York City Subway)") in the north. This extension connected to the [Fulton Street Line](/wiki/BMT_Fulton_Street_Line "BMT Fulton Street Line") of the [Kings County Elevated Railroad](/wiki/Kings_County_Elevated_Railroad "Kings County Elevated Railroad"), allowing [rapid transit](/wiki/Rapid_transit "Rapid transit") trains on Fulton Street to operate along the Brighton Line. These trains ran from Brighton Beach, up the Franklin Avenue and Fulton Street lines to the Brooklyn side of the [Brooklyn Bridge](/wiki/Brooklyn_Bridge "Brooklyn Bridge"), where walking or transferring to a [cable car](/wiki/Cable_car_(railway) "Cable car (railway)") service connection over the bridge allowed access to [New York City Hall](/wiki/New_York_City_Hall "New York City Hall") at Park Row in Manhattan. In 1900, elevated trains were through-routed to [Park Row](/wiki/Park_Row_(BMT_station) "Park Row (BMT station)") without need to change trains.[[7]](#cite_note-Derrick_2002-8): 279 By 1903, a surface extension of the Brighton Beach Line on what is now Brighton Beach Avenue permitted through service from Park Row, Manhattan west to [Culver Depot](/wiki/Culver_Depot "Culver Depot") at Surf Avenue near West 8th Street, much nearer to the growing amusement center known then as West Brighton and now as Coney Island.[[6]](#cite_note-Cudahy_1999-7): 21

In 1908, a massive grade crossing elimination project was completed with a 4-track line from south of Church Avenue station to Neptune Avenue near the [Coney Island Creek](/wiki/Coney_Island_Creek "Coney Island Creek"), permitting true local and express service, as pioneered on the New York City Subway that opened in 1904.[[6]](#cite_note-Cudahy_1999-7): 22 The Brighton Beach line was also converted to electrified third rail. Brighton Beach local and express service was extended to a new West End terminal at Stillwell and Surf Avenues, the location of the [Coney Island terminal](/wiki/Coney_Island%E2%80%93Stillwell_Avenue_(BMT_Brighton_Line) "Coney Island–Stillwell Avenue (BMT Brighton Line)") for the BMT Southern Division, in May 1919.[[8]](#cite_note-9)

### 1920–1950: Subway service begins

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Q_(New_York_City_Subway_service)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1920–1950: Subway service begins")]

On August 1, 1920, subway service on the [BMT Brighton Line](/wiki/BMT_Brighton_Line "BMT Brighton Line"), then owned by the [Brooklyn-Manhattan Transit Corporation](/wiki/Brooklyn-Manhattan_Transit_Corporation "Brooklyn-Manhattan Transit Corporation") (BMT), officially began with the openings of a two-track underground subway between Prospect Park and [DeKalb Avenue](/wiki/DeKalb_Avenue_(BMT_Fourth_Avenue_Line) "DeKalb Avenue (BMT Fourth Avenue Line)") and the [Montague Street Tunnel](/wiki/Montague_Street_Tunnel "Montague Street Tunnel") between Brooklyn and Manhattan.[[7]](#cite_note-Derrick_2002-8): 285

In 1921, PM rush hour express service was extended from Kings Highway to Brighton Beach. In 1923, Brighton express service operated via the Montague Tunnel and ran local on the Broadway Line. The BMT held a vote to see which route riders preferred on August 30, 1923. Passengers voted to have Brighton expresses run from Brighton Beach to Times Square via the Manhattan Bridge and the express tracks on the Broadway Line. This change took effect on about October 1, 1923. This subway service was labeled 1 by the BMT starting in 1924, with the remnant service to Franklin Avenue becoming the 7. 1 Brighton Express service operated during rush hours and Saturday afternoons. During the evening rush hour and on Saturday afternoons, trains skipped Canal Street.[[9]](#cite_note-Linder_2003-10)

The span of express service was extended by 90 minutes until 8:27 p.m. leaving Times Square in 1929. Express service began operating between the AM rush hour and noon on Saturday mornings in April 1930. Express service began operating middays on May 30, 1931, replacing short-line local service. In September 1937, Brighton express service ran between Brighton Beach and Times Square rush hours, middays, and early evenings weekdays and Saturdays.

During the 1930s, limited morning rush hour service ran via the south side tracks of the Manhattan Bridge and the [Nassau Street Loop](/wiki/Nassau_Street_Loop "Nassau Street Loop") to [Chambers Street](/wiki/Chambers_Street_(BMT_Nassau_Street_Line) "Chambers Street (BMT Nassau Street Line)") on the [BMT Nassau Street Line](/wiki/BMT_Nassau_Street_Line "BMT Nassau Street Line"). On June 29, 1950, trains began running there during the evening rush as well.

On October 17, 1949, the [IRT Astoria Line](/wiki/BMT_Astoria_Line "BMT Astoria Line") in Queens, up to this point operated by the [Interborough Rapid Transit](/wiki/Interborough_Rapid_Transit "Interborough Rapid Transit") (IRT), was converted to BMT operation. 1 Local trains were extended via the [60th Street Tunnel](/wiki/60th_Street_Tunnel "60th Street Tunnel") and the BMT Astoria Line to [Astoria–Ditmars Boulevard](/wiki/Astoria%E2%80%93Ditmars_Boulevard_(BMT_Astoria_Line) "Astoria–Ditmars Boulevard (BMT Astoria Line)") during weekday rush hours, and on Saturday mornings and early afternoons. Number 2 [Fourth Avenue Local](/wiki/R_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "R (New York City Subway service)") trains also ran here at all times.[[10]](#cite_note-Linder_2005-11)

### 1950s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Q_(New_York_City_Subway_service)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1950s")]

On April 27, 1950, 1 Local trains were extended to Astoria–Ditmars Boulevard during middays.[[10]](#cite_note-Linder_2005-11) On June 26, 1952, 1 Express trains were extended from Times Square to 57th Street–Seventh Avenue on weekdays after the morning rush hour, running local north of 34th Street. On June 28, 1952, special service from Brighton Beach to the Nassau Street Line was discontinued on Saturdays, and Saturday express service was extended to 57th Street.[[11]](#cite_note-12)[[9]](#cite_note-Linder_2003-10)

The [60th Street Tunnel Connection](/wiki/60th_Street_Tunnel_Connection "60th Street Tunnel Connection") opened on December 1, 1955, connecting the Broadway Line to the [IND Queens Boulevard Line](/wiki/IND_Queens_Boulevard_Line "IND Queens Boulevard Line"). 1 Local trains were rerouted to this new connector to [Forest Hills–71st Avenue](/wiki/Forest_Hills%E2%80%9371st_Avenue_(IND_Queens_Boulevard_Line) "Forest Hills–71st Avenue (IND Queens Boulevard Line)") in [Forest Hills, Queens](/wiki/Forest_Hills,_Queens "Forest Hills, Queens") between 6:30 a.m. and 8:20 p.m. They were replaced on the [BMT Astoria Line](/wiki/BMT_Astoria_Line "BMT Astoria Line") by 1 Express trains on weekdays.[[12]](#cite_note-13) On May 4, 1957, 1 Express trains running started running to Ditmars Boulevard on Saturdays as well,[[10]](#cite_note-Linder_2005-11) but made local stops in Manhattan[[9]](#cite_note-Linder_2003-10) as the local trains in Brooklyn now ran to Chambers Street via the BMT Nassau Street Line. The final portion of the Broadway Line's express tracks, between Times Square–42nd Street and 57th Street–Seventh Avenue, was placed in service on May 2, 1957. 1 Brighton Express trains ran local in Manhattan on Saturdays while Brighton Locals ran express here during evenings and on Sundays. This lasted only until the next service change. On October 24, 1957, Brighton Local trains ran via the Manhattan Bridge and local in Manhattan, all day on Sundays as well as evenings and midnight hours. Brighton Express 1 service on weekdays began using the express tracks between Times Square–42nd Street and 57th Street–Seventh Avenue.

A December 1957 strike shut down much of the BMT Division. Brighton Local 1 trains ran in two sections, from Coney Island via tunnel to 57th Street-Seventh Avenue and from [Whitehall Street](/wiki/Whitehall_Street_(BMT_Broadway_Line) "Whitehall Street (BMT Broadway Line)") to [Jamaica–179th Street](/wiki/Jamaica%E2%80%93179th_Street_(IND_Queens_Boulevard_Line) "Jamaica–179th Street (IND Queens Boulevard Line)") on the IND Queens Boulevard Line. Due to the differing unions predominating on the various divisions, the IND was completely knocked out of service, while the IRT ran virtually normal service. The BMT was about half affected, with makeshift service patterns being set up for the duration of the strike.

On May 28, 1959, 1 Brighton Express trains midday on weekdays were cut back to 57th Street–Seventh Avenue and made local stops in Brooklyn midday. Multiple trains entered service at Queensboro Plaza in the evening rush hour.[[9]](#cite_note-Linder_2003-10) Nassau Specials returned, running via the Montague Street tunnel during the morning rush and via the Manhattan Bridge during the evening rush. As part of the same service change, Brighton Local trains, beginning on June 6, ran to Franklin Avenue via the route of the [7 Shuttle](/wiki/Franklin_Avenue_Shuttle "Franklin Avenue Shuttle") on Saturdays. This was not seasonal and ran the entire day, being quite distinct from the Sunday service which still operated.

### 1960–1987: Lettered variants and Chrystie Street Connection

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Q_(New_York_City_Subway_service)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 1960–1987: Lettered variants and Chrystie Street Connection")]

On November 15, 1960, with the arrival of the [R27 subway cars](/wiki/R27_(New_York_City_Subway_car) "R27 (New York City Subway car)"), 1 service on the Brighton Line was relabeled. Brighton Express service was designated as Q, Brighton Local via the Montague St Tunnel as QT, and Brighton Local via the Manhattan Bridge as QB. Single letters were used to refer to express lines and double letters for local lines, a practice that began thirty years earlier with the [Independent Subway System](/wiki/Independent_Subway_System "Independent Subway System") (IND), however, no QQ designation was ever used. Despite these new designations, subway communications continued to refer to the services as "Brighton Local" and "Brighton Express".

Effective January 1, 1961, Q Brighton Express service was cut back from Ditmars Boulevard to 57th Street–Seventh Avenue on weekdays, with trains skipping 49th Street.[[9]](#cite_note-Linder_2003-10) Saturday daytime service continued to run to Ditmars Boulevard.[[13]](#cite_note-Flickr.com_1961-14) QT service ran to Ditmars Boulevard on weekdays; on Saturdays, it ran via the Franklin Avenue Line to Franklin Avenue in Brooklyn instead. The QB provided off-peak service between Coney Island and Astoria, via Brighton Local and the Manhattan Bridge. Sunday service between Franklin Avenue and Brighton Beach was discontinued on this date, with Sunday service now provided solely by the [Franklin Avenue Shuttle (SS, formerly 7)](/wiki/Franklin_Avenue_Shuttle "Franklin Avenue Shuttle") between Prospect Park and Fulton Street.

Service between Brighton Beach and Franklin Avenue was merged into the Franklin Avenue Shuttle service on October 14, 1961, and all non-shuttle service between was discontinued in February 1963. The [Fourth Avenue Local (RR)](/wiki/R_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "R (New York City Subway service)") now provided Broadway Line service along the Queens Boulevard line on weekdays, and the [West End Express (T)](/wiki/T_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "T (New York City Subway service)") was extended from 57th Street to Ditmars Boulevard during rush hours. This service change essentially swapped the northern terminals of the Brighton Local and RR, and between the Brighton Express and T. Prior to this both Brighton Line–Broadway services had operated via the 60th Street Tunnel to Queens. By having the Brighton Express Q terminate at 57th Street, this change served to keep one Brighton Line service unaffected in the event of a massive delay in the 60th Street Tunnel.[[13]](#cite_note-Flickr.com_1961-14)

On April 21, 1962, Saturday express Q service was discontinued, and replaced by QB service.[[14]](#cite_note-15) All Saturday trains on the Brighton Line began running local, doubling the frequency of service and providing a one-seat ride to Manhattan for riders at local stations. With the arrival of new subway cars to the line, which provided improved running times, trains making local stops between Brighton Beach and Prospect Park did so in only 1.5 minutes longer than existing express service.[[15]](#cite_note-16)

From February 10 to November 2, 1964, the Brighton Express tracks were closed to permit platform extension work at Newkirk Avenue. [Skip-stop](/wiki/Skip-stop "Skip-stop") service was instituted along the Brighton Line. Brighton Express service, which made A stops, ran express from Brighton Beach to Kings Highway, and then stopped at Avenue J, Newkirk Avenue, Cortelyou Road, Beverley Road, Church Avenue, Parkside Avenue, and Prospect Park.[[9]](#cite_note-Linder_2003-10)

On November 26, 1967, the [Chrystie Street Connection](/wiki/Chrystie_Street_Connection "Chrystie Street Connection") opened, connecting the Brighton Line in Brooklyn to the [IND Sixth Avenue Line](/wiki/IND_Sixth_Avenue_Line "IND Sixth Avenue Line") in Manhattan via the [Manhattan Bridge](/wiki/Manhattan_Bridge "Manhattan Bridge"). The bridge's south side tracks, which formerly connected to the [BMT Nassau Street Line](/wiki/BMT_Nassau_Street_Line "BMT Nassau Street Line"), were now connected to the Broadway Line express tracks instead. The bridge's north side tracks, which formerly connected to the Broadway Line, now connected to the Sixth Avenue Line express tracks. Originally, the [D](/wiki/D_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "D (New York City Subway service)") running via [Sixth Avenue Express](/wiki/IND_Sixth_Avenue_Line "IND Sixth Avenue Line"), and the [QJ](/wiki/QJ_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "QJ (New York City Subway service)") running via [Nassau Street](/wiki/BMT_Nassau_Street_Line "BMT Nassau Street Line") and the [Montague Street Tunnel](/wiki/Montague_Street_Tunnel "Montague Street Tunnel"), were to replace all three Q services on the Brighton Line. The Q and QT went out of existence completely, but due to riders' opposition to the expected loss of all Broadway Line service, some QB trains were retained, now running rush hours only in the peak direction between Coney Island and 57th Street via Brighton Local, the Manhattan Bridge, and Broadway Express in Manhattan. The color scheme introduced for subway lines that day included a red QB bullet. A short-lived [NX](/wiki/N_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "N (New York City Subway service)") service also provided rush-hour service between Brighton Beach and Coney Island and the Broadway Line, running via the [BMT Sea Beach Line](/wiki/BMT_Sea_Beach_Line "BMT Sea Beach Line") to Manhattan. This service was discontinued on April 15, 1968, after less than five months.[[16]](#cite_note-17) The RR replaced Q, QB and QT service to Astoria–Ditmars Boulevard.[[10]](#cite_note-Linder_2005-11)

On August 19, 1968, one AM rush hour QB train began running to Ditmars Boulevard. From January 2, 1973, no QB trains ran in service to Ditmars Boulevard, though two trains ran light to Ditmars Boulevard from 57th Street in the AM rush, and one train ran light to 57th Street in the PM rush. On January 19, 1976, morning rush hour QB trains began running in service to Ditmars Boulevard, and most evening rush hour trains entered service at Queensboro Plaza, with only one evening rush hour train running from Coney Island to 57th Street. All but the first QB morning QB trip, which entered RR service, were cut back from Ditmars Boulevard to 57th Street on August 30, 1976. Evening rush hour trains only ran in service between Coney Island and 57th Street. The last PM rush hour QB train started at Ditmars Boulevard, having previously made a trip in RR service. The first two morning rush hour QB trains ran to Ditmars Boulevard as of May 7, 1978, returning in service as RR trains to 36th Street. The last two evening rush hour QB trains entered service at Ditmars Boulevard, with the final trip having previously made an RR trip from 36th Street.[[17]](#cite_note-18)[[10]](#cite_note-Linder_2005-11)
In 1979, the MTA released a revised coloring scheme for subway routes based on trunk line; the QB service was assigned the color sunflower yellow, with black text, because it used the [BMT Broadway Line](/wiki/BMT_Broadway_Line "BMT Broadway Line") in Manhattan. It now used a diamond-shaped bullet because it ran rush hours only.[[18]](#cite_note-Hertz-Gothamist-19)[[19]](#cite_note-color-20) On May 5, 1985, the double-letter naming scheme for local services was dropped; the QB was renamed the Q the next day.[[20]](#cite_note-21)

Starting on April 26, 1986, the Brighton Line's local tracks underwent reconstruction between Prospect Park and [Newkirk Avenue](/wiki/Newkirk_Plaza_(BMT_Brighton_Line) "Newkirk Plaza (BMT Brighton Line)"), requiring the suspension of express service; at the same time, reconstruction of the [Manhattan Bridge](/wiki/Manhattan_Bridge "Manhattan Bridge") started, which would disrupt subway service until 2004. QB service was discontinued.[[10]](#cite_note-Linder_2005-11) From April 28, 1986 to May 23, 1987, one AM rush hour train from Brighton Beach was extended beyond its normal terminal at 57th Street/Seventh Avenue and terminated at [Forest Hills – 71st Avenue](/wiki/Forest_Hills_%E2%80%93_71st_Avenue_(IND_Queens_Boulevard_Line) "Forest Hills – 71st Avenue (IND Queens Boulevard Line)").[[21]](#cite_note-22)

The bridge's north side tracks (leading to the Sixth Avenue Line) closed. The Q now ran rush hours between 57th Street–Seventh Avenue and Brighton Beach, using a yellow diamond bullet. Because the Manhattan Bridge's north side tracks closed, the D and Q ran on the bridge's south side tracks, both running via Broadway Express to 57th Street–Seventh Avenue. To substitute for the suspended Brighton Line express service, the Q ran [skip-stop](/wiki/Skip-stop "Skip-stop") service with the D between Newkirk Avenue and [Sheepshead Bay](/wiki/Sheepshead_Bay_(BMT_Brighton_Line) "Sheepshead Bay (BMT Brighton Line)"). D trains served [Neck Road](/wiki/Neck_Road_(BMT_Brighton_Line) "Neck Road (BMT Brighton Line)"), [Avenue M](/wiki/Avenue_M_(BMT_Brighton_Line) "Avenue M (BMT Brighton Line)") and [Avenue H](/wiki/Avenue_H_(BMT_Brighton_Line) "Avenue H (BMT Brighton Line)"); the Q skipped those stops, serving [Avenue U](/wiki/Avenue_U_(BMT_Brighton_Line) "Avenue U (BMT Brighton Line)") and [Avenue J](/wiki/Avenue_J_(BMT_Brighton_Line) "Avenue J (BMT Brighton Line)"), while both lines served [Kings Highway](/wiki/Kings_Highway_(BMT_Brighton_Line) "Kings Highway (BMT Brighton Line)").[[22]](#cite_note-23) By 1987, as reconstruction on the Brighton Line progressed, the weekday skip-stop pattern expanded to Prospect Park, with D trains serving [Beverley Road](/wiki/Beverley_Road_(BMT_Brighton_Line) "Beverley Road (BMT Brighton Line)") while Q trains served [Cortelyou Road](/wiki/Cortelyou_Road_(BMT_Brighton_Line) "Cortelyou Road (BMT Brighton Line)") and [Parkside Avenue](/wiki/Parkside_Avenue_(BMT_Brighton_Line) "Parkside Avenue (BMT Brighton Line)"), with [Church Avenue](/wiki/Church_Avenue_(BMT_Brighton_Line) "Church Avenue (BMT Brighton Line)") as a mutual station.[[23]](#cite_note-24)

### 1988–2004: Manhattan Bridge reconstruction

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Q_(New_York_City_Subway_service)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 1988–2004: Manhattan Bridge reconstruction")]

#### 1988–2001: Sixth Avenue service

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Q_(New_York_City_Subway_service)&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 1988–2001: Sixth Avenue service")]

On December 11, 1988, the Bridge's north side tracks reopened and the south side tracks closed, and the reconstruction project on the Brighton Line ended. The Q became the weekday Brighton Express to Brighton Beach and was rerouted via the north side of the bridge and the [IND Sixth Avenue Line](/wiki/IND_Sixth_Avenue_Line "IND Sixth Avenue Line") to [57th Street–Sixth Avenue](/wiki/57th_Street_(IND_Sixth_Avenue_Line) "57th Street (IND Sixth Avenue Line)"), [Midtown Manhattan](/wiki/Midtown_Manhattan "Midtown Manhattan"). Because it ran on the Sixth Avenue Line in Manhattan, the route now used an orange bullet on maps. One AM rush hour train from Brighton Beach terminated at [168th Street](/wiki/168th_Street_(IND_Eighth_Avenue_Line) "168th Street (IND Eighth Avenue Line)") in Manhattan instead of 21st Street; this trip last ran on September 28, 1990.[[9]](#cite_note-Linder_2003-10)

On October 29, 1989, the [IND 63rd Street Line](/wiki/63rd_Street_Lines "63rd Street Lines") opened and the B, Q, and [JFK Express](/wiki/JFK_Express "JFK Express") were extended to [21st Street–Queensbridge](/wiki/21st_Street%E2%80%93Queensbridge_(IND_63rd_Street_Line) "21st Street–Queensbridge (IND 63rd Street Line)") in [Long Island City](/wiki/Long_Island_City,_Queens "Long Island City, Queens"). Weekday evening service terminated at [Broadway–Lafayette Street](/wiki/Broadway%E2%80%93Lafayette_Street_(IND_Sixth_Avenue_Line) "Broadway–Lafayette Street (IND Sixth Avenue Line)") in Manhattan instead of Brooklyn; these trains relayed at [Second Avenue](/wiki/Second_Avenue_(IND_Sixth_Avenue_Line) "Second Avenue (IND Sixth Avenue Line)") in order to change direction. A special combined [F](/wiki/F_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "F (New York City Subway service)")–Q service ran during late nights; in the northbound direction, F trains would operate along its normal route from Coney Island to [47th–50th Streets–Rockefeller Center](/wiki/47th%E2%80%9350th_Streets%E2%80%93Rockefeller_Center_(IND_Sixth_Avenue_Line) "47th–50th Streets–Rockefeller Center (IND Sixth Avenue Line)"), then turn into a Q and operate to [21st Street–Queensbridge](/wiki/21st_Street%E2%80%93Queensbridge_(IND_63rd_Street_Line) "21st Street–Queensbridge (IND 63rd Street Line)"); in the southbound direction, Q trains would operate from 21st Street to 47th–50th Streets, then turn into an F train and operate along its normal route to Coney Island.[[24]](#cite_note-25) The weekday evening shuttle was replaced by the B on September 30, 1990. The [F](/wiki/F_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "F (New York City Subway service)") replaced the late night shuttle in April 1993.[[25]](#cite_note-26)

In January 1991, a reduction of service along the Central Park West corridor to remove excess capacity was proposed. Initially, Q service would operate between [207th Street](/wiki/Inwood%E2%80%93207th_Street_(IND_Eighth_Avenue_Line) "Inwood–207th Street (IND Eighth Avenue Line)") and [Brighton Beach](/wiki/Brighton_Beach_(BMT_Brighton_Line) "Brighton Beach (BMT Brighton Line)") during weekday rush hours and middays, making express stops along its entire route. The service plan was later amended to eliminate the Q designation and replace it with an orange [A](/wiki/A_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "A (New York City Subway service)"), assuming the same service pattern that was proposed for the Q. This service change would have been implemented in October 1991, pending approval from the MTA board.[[26]](#cite_note-27)

On February 6, 1995, Q trains began running local south of Kings Highway due to rehabilitation work on the Brighton Line.[[9]](#cite_note-Linder_2003-10) On April 30, 1995, the north side of the [Manhattan Bridge](/wiki/Manhattan_Bridge "Manhattan Bridge") closed during middays and weekends, in addition to the already-closed south side. During these hours, D service was cut below 34th Street–Herald Square. In its place, the Q ran between Coney Island and 21st Street–Queensbridge, via Brighton Local, the [Montague Street Tunnel](/wiki/Montague_Street_Tunnel "Montague Street Tunnel"), Broadway Express (switching between the local and express tracks at [Canal Street](/wiki/Canal_Street_(BMT_Broadway_Line) "Canal Street (BMT Broadway Line)")) and the [BMT 63rd Street Line](/wiki/63rd_Street_Lines "63rd Street Lines"). Rush hour and evening service was unchanged. On May 1, Q expresses only operated during rush hours and early evening. Normal service resumed on November 12, 1995,[[27]](#cite_note-28) including the restoration of Q express service between Kings Highway and Brighton Beach.[[28]](#cite_note-29)[[9]](#cite_note-Linder_2003-10)

On February 22, 1998, construction on the [IND 63rd Street Line](/wiki/63rd_Street_Lines "63rd Street Lines") cut [B](/wiki/B_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "B (New York City Subway service)") and Q service back to 57th Street–Sixth Avenue. Service on the 63rd Street Line was replaced by a [shuttle](/wiki/63rd_Street_Shuttle "63rd Street Shuttle") to the BMT Broadway Line at 57th Street–Seventh Avenue. Normal service resumed on May 22, 1999.[[29]](#cite_note-30)

#### 2001–2004: Brighton Express/Local Q service

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Q_(New_York_City_Subway_service)&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 2001–2004: Brighton Express/Local Q service")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/Westinghouse_R68_%22Q%22_Train_leaving_Coney_Island-Stillwell_Avenue_-_August_2023.jpg/250px-Westinghouse_R68_%22Q%22_Train_leaving_Coney_Island-Stillwell_Avenue_-_August_2023.jpg)](/wiki/File:Westinghouse_R68_%22Q%22_Train_leaving_Coney_Island-Stillwell_Avenue_-_August_2023.jpg)

A [Westinghouse R68](/wiki/R68_(New_York_City_Subway_car) "R68 (New York City Subway car)") northbound Q train leaving [Coney Island-Stillwell Avenue](/wiki/Coney_Island%E2%80%93Stillwell_Avenue_station "Coney Island–Stillwell Avenue station")

On July 22, 2001, the [Manhattan Bridge](/wiki/Manhattan_Bridge "Manhattan Bridge")'s north side tracks closed and the south side tracks reopened. There were now two Q services, colored yellow as they now ran via Broadway. In Brooklyn, the circle Q (Q local) replaced the [D](/wiki/D_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "D (New York City Subway service)") as the full-time Brighton Local to Stillwell Avenue while the <Q> (Q express or Q diamond) replaced the Sixth Avenue Q as the weekday-only Brighton Express to Brighton Beach. Both Qs used the south side of the Manhattan Bridge to travel into Manhattan and then ran to 57th Street–Seventh Avenue via Broadway Express.[[30]](#cite_note-31) Service on the IND 63rd Street Line was replaced by a [shuttle](/wiki/63rd_Street_Shuttle "63rd Street Shuttle"), which would be permanently replaced by the [F](/wiki/F_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "F (New York City Subway service)") in December 2001 once the 63rd Street's connection to the [IND Queens Boulevard Line](/wiki/IND_Queens_Boulevard_Line "IND Queens Boulevard Line") opened.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Manha_Bound_Advent_3DD03176-A20C-496D-8CD1-0F8A50557017.jpg/250px-Manha_Bound_Advent_3DD03176-A20C-496D-8CD1-0F8A50557017.jpg)](/wiki/File:Manha_Bound_Advent_3DD03176-A20C-496D-8CD1-0F8A50557017.jpg)

A [Kawasaki R68A](/wiki/R68A_(New_York_City_Subway_car) "R68A (New York City Subway car)") northbound Q train leaving [Sheepshead Bay](/wiki/Sheepshead_Bay_station "Sheepshead Bay station")

After the [September 11, 2001](/wiki/September_11_attacks "September 11 attacks") attacks, Q service was initially extended beyond 57th Street–Seventh Avenue and originated and terminated at [21st Street–Queensbridge](/wiki/21st_Street%E2%80%93Queensbridge_(IND_63rd_Street_Line) "21st Street–Queensbridge (IND 63rd Street Line)") via [63rd Street](/wiki/63rd_Street_Lines "63rd Street Lines"), but was cut back to its original terminal by the evening of September 12. On September 17, Q service was once again extended beyond 57th Street, this time originating and terminating at [Forest Hills–71st Avenue](/wiki/Forest_Hills%E2%80%9371st_Avenue_(IND_Queens_Boulevard_Line) "Forest Hills–71st Avenue (IND Queens Boulevard Line)") via [60th Street](/wiki/60th_Street_Tunnel "60th Street Tunnel") to replace the R, and made all stops in Queens, Manhattan and Brooklyn; overnight service [short turned](/wiki/Short_turn "Short turn") at 57th Street and did not operate to or from 71st Avenue. <Q> Brighton Express service continued to operate its normal service pattern.[[31]](#cite_note-32)[[32]](#cite_note-33) Normal Q service was restored on October 28.[[33]](#cite_note-34) On September 8, 2002, Coney Island–Stillwell Avenue (the Q's southern terminal) was closed for reconstruction and the Q local terminated at Brighton Beach. During this time, service at stations between Brighton Beach and Stillwell Avenue was replaced by an extension of the [B68](/wiki/List_of_bus_routes_in_Brooklyn#B68 "List of bus routes in Brooklyn") bus. Q service to Stillwell Avenue resumed on May 23, 2004.[[34]](#cite_note-35)[[35]](#cite_note-mta.info_facts-36)

From April 27[[36]](#cite_note-37) to November 2, 2003, the south side of the Manhattan Bridge was closed on weekends and Q service was rerouted via the Montague Street Tunnel.

On February 22, 2004, reconstruction of the [Manhattan Bridge](/wiki/Manhattan_Bridge "Manhattan Bridge") was completed and the north side tracks reopened. The <Q> express was discontinued and replaced with the [B](/wiki/B_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "B (New York City Subway service)") in Brooklyn and [N](/wiki/N_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "N (New York City Subway service)") in Manhattan to combine two weekday-only routes. The Q local remained unchanged.[[37]](#cite_note-38)[[38]](#cite_note-39)[[39]](#cite_note-40)

### 2004–present: Extensions to Astoria and Second Avenue

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Q_(New_York_City_Subway_service)&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 2004–present: Extensions to Astoria and Second Avenue")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c3/Second_Avenue_Subway_Map_vc.jpg/250px-Second_Avenue_Subway_Map_vc.jpg)](/wiki/File:Second_Avenue_Subway_Map_vc.jpg)

Map of the full Second Avenue Subway (SAS), showing the planned uptown portion of the Q, which currently terminates at 96th Street. Note that the section between Harlem–125th Street and Broadway is not included.

On June 28, 2010, the Q was extended from [57th Street–Seventh Avenue](/wiki/57th_Street%E2%80%93Seventh_Avenue_(BMT_Broadway_Line) "57th Street–Seventh Avenue (BMT Broadway Line)") to [Astoria–Ditmars Boulevard](/wiki/Astoria%E2%80%93Ditmars_Boulevard_(BMT_Astoria_Line) "Astoria–Ditmars Boulevard (BMT Astoria Line)") via the [60th Street Tunnel](/wiki/60th_Street_Tunnel "60th Street Tunnel") and [BMT Astoria Line](/wiki/BMT_Astoria_Line "BMT Astoria Line") on weekdays, stopping at [49th Street](/wiki/49th_Street_(BMT_Broadway_Line) "49th Street (BMT Broadway Line)").[[40]](#cite_note-41) The extended Q replaced the [W](/wiki/W_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "W (New York City Subway service)"), which was discontinued due to budget shortfalls.[[41]](#cite_note-Grynbaum_2017_r644-42)[[42]](#cite_note-43)

On December 7, 2014, late night Q service began operating local in Manhattan between 57th Street and [Canal Street](/wiki/Canal_Street_(BMT_Broadway_Line) "Canal Street (BMT Broadway Line)") in order to decrease waiting time at the local stations.[[43]](#cite_note-44)[[44]](#cite_note-45)

On November 7, 2016, weekday Q service was cut back from Astoria to 57th Street–Seventh Avenue, skipping 49th Street, to provide a seamless transition for the opening of the Second Avenue Subway.[[45]](#cite_note-Rivoli_2016_g433-46)[[46]](#cite_note-47) Service to Astoria and the 49th Street station was replaced by the restored W service.[[47]](#cite_note-48)[[48]](#cite_note-49)

On January 1, 2017, the first phase of the [Second Avenue Subway](/wiki/Second_Avenue_Subway "Second Avenue Subway") opened; the Q was extended from 57th Street–Seventh Avenue to [96th Street](/wiki/96th_Street_(Second_Avenue_Subway) "96th Street (Second Avenue Subway)") via the [BMT 63rd Street Line](/wiki/63rd_Street_Lines "63rd Street Lines") and the IND Second Avenue Line. This extension serves [Lexington Avenue–63rd Street](/wiki/Lexington_Avenue%E2%80%9363rd_Street_(63rd_Street_Lines) "Lexington Avenue–63rd Street (63rd Street Lines)") station with a [cross-platform transfer](/wiki/Cross-platform_interchange "Cross-platform interchange") to the [IND 63rd Street Line](/wiki/63rd_Street_Lines "63rd Street Lines") (served by the [F](/wiki/F_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "F (New York City Subway service)") and ​[M](/wiki/M_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "M (New York City Subway service)") trains) before serving new stations under Second Avenue at [72nd Street](/wiki/72nd_Street_(Second_Avenue_Subway) "72nd Street (Second Avenue Subway)"), [86th Street](/wiki/86th_Street_(Second_Avenue_Subway) "86th Street (Second Avenue Subway)"), and 96th Street, where it originates/terminates.[[49]](#cite_note-50) The inaugural train on the Second Avenue Line ran on December 31, 2016, with passenger service beginning the next day. From January 1 to 9, 2017, service between 57th Street and 96th Street ran only from 6 a.m. to 10 p.m., with late-night service terminating at 57th Street; late night service to 96th Street began on January 9.[[50]](#cite_note-SAS_opening-51)[[51]](#cite_note-52)

Effective June 30, 2024, one evening PM train started terminating at Brighton Beach.[[52]](#cite_note-53)[[53]](#cite_note-54)

### Future

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Q_(New_York_City_Subway_service)&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Future")]

The second phase of the Second Avenue Line will [extend](/wiki/Construction_of_the_Second_Avenue_Subway#Phase_2 "Construction of the Second Avenue Subway") the Q to a new northern terminal at [Harlem–125th Street](/wiki/Harlem%E2%80%93125th_Street_(IND_Second_Avenue_Line) "Harlem–125th Street (IND Second Avenue Line)"), with planned stops at [116th Street](/wiki/116th_Street_(Second_Avenue_Subway) "116th Street (Second Avenue Subway)") and [106th Street](/wiki/106th_Street_(Second_Avenue_Subway) "106th Street (Second Avenue Subway)"). At the Harlem–125th Street terminus, there will be a transfer to the existing [125th Street](/wiki/125th_Street_(IRT_Lexington_Avenue_Line) "125th Street (IRT Lexington Avenue Line)") station on the [IRT Lexington Avenue Line](/wiki/IRT_Lexington_Avenue_Line "IRT Lexington Avenue Line") and a connection to [Harlem–125th Street station](/wiki/Harlem%E2%80%93125th_Street_station_(Metro-North) "Harlem–125th Street station (Metro-North)") on [Metro-North Railroad](/wiki/Metro-North_Railroad "Metro-North Railroad"). This will provide residents of [East Harlem](/wiki/East_Harlem,_Manhattan "East Harlem, Manhattan") with direct subway service to the [Upper East Side](/wiki/Upper_East_Side "Upper East Side"), western [Midtown](/wiki/Midtown_Manhattan "Midtown Manhattan"), [Lower Manhattan](/wiki/Lower_Manhattan "Lower Manhattan") and [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn"), and offer connections to [4](/wiki/4_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "4 (New York City Subway service)"), ​[5](/wiki/5_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "5 (New York City Subway service)"), ​[6](/wiki/6_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "6 (New York City Subway service)"), and [<6>](/wiki/6d_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "6d (New York City Subway service)") trains and Metro-North from [the Bronx](/wiki/The_Bronx "The Bronx"), the northern suburbs of New York City, and southern [Connecticut](/wiki/Connecticut "Connecticut").[[54]](#cite_note-55) An extension of the Second Avenue Line further west under 125th Street has been proposed, with transfers to other routes, although this extension has not been funded or approved.[[55]](#cite_note-Campbell_Nessen_2024_l686-56)[[56]](#cite_note-57)
